title: Composting wood chips: breaking down high-carbon woody material
subtitle: Managing the C:N ratio and moisture to get wood chips composting rather than locking up nitrogen
excerpt: Wood chips from a shredder or arborist are a useful carbon source for compost, but a pile of raw chips alone composts very slowly and can immobilise soil nitrogen around them for months. With correct C:N management, moisture, and a nitrogen activator, wood chips transform into a rich fibrous compost in six to twelve months.
sourceNotes: USDA Forest Service guidance on wood chip composting and carbon sequestration. RHS guidance on composting woody material. Jeff Lowenfels 'Teaming with Microbes' for soil biology context (referenced for mechanism, not verbatim). Arborist chip research from Cornell University Extension (public domain publications).

glossaryTerms.definition: The carbon-to-nitrogen ratio in compost or organic material. A ratio of 25-30:1 is ideal for hot composting. Kitchen scraps are typically nitrogen-rich (low ratio); wood chips and straw are carbon-rich (high ratio).
Active thermophilic composting where the heap reaches 55-70°C, killing weed seeds and pathogens and accelerating decomposition to weeks rather than months.

content.text: of roughly 400-500:1. The target for active hot composting is 25-30:1. Adding a bag of fresh wood chips to a working heap slows or stops decomposition until bacteria have broken down the carbon-rich lignin and cellulose. Worse, if wood chips are worked into soil directly, soil bacteria colonise them and temporarily pull nitrogen out of the soil solution in the process of breaking them down, leaving nearby plants nitrogen-deficient for months.
Three approaches to composting wood chips

Pile the chips in a heap at least 1 m³ in volume. Keep moist (wrung-out sponge texture). Turn once or twice per year. No nitrogen addition needed: fungi colonise the pile and slowly break down the lignin over 12-24 months. The result is a fibrous, dark-brown material suitable for mulch or soil amendment. The cold method requires the least management and produces a stable, slightly acidic material that suits acid-loving plants well.
Approach 2: hot composting with nitrogen activator (3-6 months)

Blend the chips with a high-nitrogen activator to bring the C:N ratio toward 30:1. For every 4 buckets of wood chips, add 1 bucket of fresh grass clippings, or 0.5 kg of blood meal, or 2 litres of urine. Build the pile to at least 1 m³. Keep moist. The pile should reach 50-60°C within three to five days; turn when the temperature drops below 40°C. With three to four turns, the material is composted in three to six months. Check with a compost thermometer.
Approach 3: direct mulch on no-dig beds (never incorporated)

Fresh wood chips, particularly from an arborist (a mix of chipped trunk, branch, bark, and leaf material), can be applied as surface mulch on no-dig beds at a depth of 100-150 mm. The surface layer is colonised by fungi, which break down the wood and do not contact the root zone below. Nutrients released from the chips wash down slowly over 18-24 months. Do not dig chips in; keep them on the surface. This is the 'Back to Eden' mulch method; evidence for it is primarily observational rather than controlled.
Types of wood chips to avoid
Treated timber: decking and fence posts often contain preservatives or creosote. These survive composting and can damage soil life.
Black walnut: juglone, a natural compound in black walnut, suppresses growth in many vegetables and ornamentals. This effect persists even after partial composting.
Chips from trees with fire blight or canker: the fungal or bacterial pathogen may survive cold composting.

Wood chips dry out quickly and stop decomposing when dry. The pile should feel moist throughout when squeezed: the wrung-out sponge test (moisture is present but no water drips when you squeeze a handful). In dry summers, cover the heap with a tarpaulin or water it weekly. Overly wet chips develop anaerobic zones that smell and produce little compost; ensure some drainage at the base of the pile.

attrs.symptom: Chip pile is not heating up after adding nitrogen activator
attrs.cause: Pile is too small (less than 1 m³), too dry, or the nitrogen source was insufficient
attrs.fix: Check moisture first. If dry, water thoroughly. If moisture is fine, add another nitrogen source and turn to mix. A pile under 1 m³ rarely reaches thermophilic temperatures regardless of C:N ratio.

attrs.symptom: Chips smell of ammonia after turning
attrs.cause: Too much nitrogen relative to carbon; the excess nitrogen is being lost as ammonia gas
attrs.fix: Add more carbon: dry leaves, shredded cardboard, or more dry wood chips. Turn to mix.
